turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-1141, Smith & Wesson Brands v. Estados Unidos Mexicanos.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mexico has a serious gun violence problem which its government views as coming from north of the border. Mexico says that gun traffickers purchase firearms in the United States, very often in illegal transactions, and deliver them to drug cartels in Mexico, where they are used to commit the worst kinds of crimes, including murder and kidnapping. Wanting to hold someone in this country accountable, Mexico brought this civil lawsuit against six American gun manufacturers, among them Colt, Glock, and Smith & Wesson. The basic theory of the suit is that those companies should pay damages for all the harm their products cause in Mexico when cartel members use them. But Mexico's suit faces a problem right at the outset. A few decades ago, American cities tried to bring similar suits against gun manufacturers, and Congress passed a law to prevent them. The main provision of that law, whose acronym is PLCAA, says that no one can sue a firearms manufacturer for injuries that stem from the criminal or unlawful misuse of guns by a third party. A third party like a Mexican cartel member. So, the statute would seem to bar Mexico's action. But, PLCAA has an exception, which Mexico says allows its suit to go forward. The exception applies when, among other things, the defendant manufacturer has, and I am quoting here, "when the manufacturer has knowingly violated a state or federal statute applicable to the sale or marketing of firearms." Mexico says the defendant manufacturers in its suit meet that requirement because they aided and abetted some dealers in their distribution chain to illegally sell firearms to Mexican gun traffickers. The question for us is whether Mexico plausibly pleaded that allegation? We hold that it did not, so its lawsuit must be dismissed. The bulk of our opinion has to do with aiding and abetting law. To aid and abet a crime, a person or entity, as the case may be, must intend to facilitate the offense and must act to do so. A canonical statement of what that means is that a person must participate in the crime as in something that he wishes to bring about and must seek by his action to make the crime succeed. Inactions and omissions usually aren't enough, nor is it enough if the person or entity engages in routine activity that happens on occasion, incidentally, if you will, to assist a crime. So, a business doesn't become liable for all criminal misuses of its goods, even if it knows that in some fraction of cases that kind of downstream use will in fact occur. Those principles mean that Mexico's suit can't succeed. It's not enough for aiding and abetting liability to say, as Mexico has, that the manufacturers are aware that some proportion of their dealer network violates firearms laws. And it's not enough to say, as Mexico has, that the manufacturers could have taken proactive steps to prevent that from happening, for example, by supervising their dealers more closely. It could be enough if the manufacturers knew who the rogue dealers were and did something to help along their gun violations, but Mexico has not plausibly pleaded that allegation. It has claimed it to be true, but in a wholly non-specific way and without anything to back up the charge. And even at this early stage of litigation, when we don't expect a plaintiff to have a lot of its evidence, even at this early stage, that kind of simple speculation cannot save Mexico's suit. The opinion of the court is unanimous. Justice Thomas and Justice Jackson have filed concurring opinions.
